speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's the 15th of April, 1996. The survivor is, Michael Sheridan. Interviewer is Susie Philips. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the language this morning will be English. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Susie Phillips. And we are interviewing liberator, Michael Sheridan, who was born Michael Shernovsky. The date is April the 15th, 1996. And the interview is in Sydney, Australia. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, could you please introduce yourself to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name's Michael Sheridan.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in London, 2nd of November, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What does that make me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That makes me 77, or 78, 77, 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: 78 young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Young, yes. 78, young. Thank you. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your family background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in a district called Whitechapel, in London. I was born to Mother and Father, came from, originally, from Poland. My father was a butcher. My mother was a normal Jewish housewife. And I'm one of seven children. I am a twin. I have two sisters and four brothers. Three of my brothers are now demised. I went to a normal elementary school in London, and left school when I was 14 years of age. I initially helped my father in his-- in his butcher shop. But I didn't like that, as I was interested in electronic-- radio at that time. I went to work for a radio firm in Hackney, London. During that time, I became a Young Communist. And I belonged to a Young Communist League, mainly because of the rabid anti-Semitism that was preached by the British Union of Fascists at that time. And I think, mostly young Jewish boys who I was surrounded by or lived near us, we all joined the Young Communist League, because it was mainly the party that opposed Oswald Mosely at the time. During that time, the Spanish Civil War broke out in 1936. I wanted to volunteer to go to Spain, but my mother put her foot down on that right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a premonition that war was going to break out with Germany, mainly because of the Spanish Civil War. And I volunteered and joined the British Army. It was the 22nd of March, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go on, I'll just go back to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I noted that your brothers and sisters all had different names. Why did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think mainly because we didn't want to be recognized as Jews at the time, being Jewish. There was a lot of that-- well, a lot of Jewish-- anti-Semitism was being preached by the British Union of Fascists at the time. And sometimes, it could have been difficult trying to get a job, or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being embarrassed, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't change my name until, actually, I was in the Army. And I thought that if I was going abroad to fight, I didn't want to have the name of Shernovsky, in case I got captured by the Germans, which seems a little unreasonable right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, I did join the Army on 27th of March, 1939. During one of my leaves, I married my late wife, which was 12th of July, 1939. I did home service right till the 3rd of June, 1944. Then left from Harich for D-Day. I landed on Arromanches--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hang on. You're going too fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a bit. You suffered anti-Semitism. You yourself suffered anti-Semitism growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not too much. As I said, in a very not abstract way, but a very covert way-- covert way, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say this party was getting quite strong in Great Britain, the British Union of Fascists. The present government at the time, which I think was the Baldwin government, seemed to turn a blind eye to this anti-Semitism. And I was involved in lots of clashes, not personally. But a Young Communist League and Jewish people were generally assailed with anti-Semitism and snide remarks. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any remarks made to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Go back to-- go back to Palestine, Jew boy, Yid, things like that, which-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was very, very-- what? Very, very painful at the time, because was only young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was a kosher butcher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father was a kosher butcher. He worked for the Jewish [INAUDIBLE] in London, the butcher-- butchering side of the [INAUDIBLE]. And after a while, he did open his own business. [? Well Street, ?] Hackney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the family observant then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My-- my-- both my mother and father were very, very Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to shul or participate in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yeah. Yes, I-- we carried out the high holidays very strictly, my father did. I had-- I was bar mitzvahed same time as my brother, my twin brother. And I suppose, after a time, I came out of the Army, or before I came out of the Army, I was pretty observant. I'm a little agnostic at the moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were some of your hobbies and interests as you were growing up as a young-- young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was quite good at drawing and art. I was interested in radio. I took that up as a hobby. And I did quite a bit of cycling and swimming, which were my hobbies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I belonged to a Young Communist League. I belonged to a Young Socialist League. [INAUDIBLE]. I belonged to the Boy Scouts. I also belonged to two cycling clubs. One was called the Polytechnic, which was quite a well-known, famous cycling club in England, or London at the time. And then I formed my own cycling club, called the [? Aurent ?] Road Club. And I kept on cycling, I suppose, till I was about 17 or 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Competitive cycling? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did competitive cycling. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you belong to any other youth organizations, religious organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I belonged to the [? Harbonny. ?] I was a member of the [? Harbonny. ?] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get your Communist ideas from? From home, or from the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the only party at the time, what I recollect. My older brother was a-- also a Communist, mainly because of the training at the time, the tension at the time, with all those workers might have been debilitated a lot in England, you know, with the mines and things like that. And I think, mostly, young Jewish boys, maybe even girls, at that time, they were, I think, quite idealistic about what was happening. And I think we only saw some sort of-- we championed, I think, the Communist party, maybe, because it seemed to be the only party in England at the time that was prepared to fight fascism at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you idealistic towards Israel or Palestine in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never knew about Israel. And Palestine was just a distant land, as far as I was concerned. I don't think it was a case of being Jewish. It was mainly we were socialists. And what was happening again, in Spain, and what was happening in England as well, I think we found the only salvation we really had was to belong to a party that was prepared to fight the fascists at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your-- how did your parents feel about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think most parents at the time, especially they came from the [INAUDIBLE], from Poland and Russia, did attach really any importance to it, mainly because, I suppose, they were glad to have come to England, you know? They were allowed to come in in England as immigrants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they speak with an accent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Both my mother and father did. Yeah. Till-- till-- right to their dying day, I don't think they ever spoke perfect English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What aspirations did you have growing up, before you left school? What did you want to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know what I wanted to be at that time when jobs were a little scarce, I think. If they weren't scarce, I couldn't really make up my mind. Although, I was always interested in radio and, you know, mechanical things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dreams? Yeah. I dreamed of getting married, being-- becoming a rich man, but [LAUGHS] it didn't always turn out like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left school at 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I left school at 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to work for your father for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, went to work for a small radio firm at the time, because my father hadn't opened up his own shop then. But when he did open up his own shop, he asked me to help him in the shop. And I did it very, very reluctantly. My heart wasn't in it to be a butcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do after you left? Did you stay with the radio business for a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then I went to work for a radio shop in Hackney, London. And I think I stayed for most of the time, right till the time I joined the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before you joined the Army, were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was courting.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married 12th of July, 1939 at Westcliff-on-Sea, Essex.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you joined the Army before? After?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I joined the Army-- yeah, I got married whilst I was in the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had two children also before-- during the war period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, My eldest son, David, was born 1940. And my daughter was born 1942. My youngest son was born 1954, after I came out of the services.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you join the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I believe I gave you my reason why I joined, mainly because I thought it was a way of fighting fascism and Hitlerism. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I suppose being idealistic about these matters, you know? So especially because I was Jewish. I mean, it was quite a number of my friends who joined the Army at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your wife feel about you joining the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't-- I wasn't married when I joined the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were courting her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were courting her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was quite amazed, I think. She thought it was a little-- not stupid, but a little, maybe, naive. I don't know. But I think the woman's feelings are always different about these kind of things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I say-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spent the rest of my Army period, till 1944, in the UK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what you did in the Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a machine gunner. I joined the Middlesex Regiment initially. I was a machine gunner. And what we mainly did was infantry training and thing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You always stayed in the same area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we moved quite a few times. I-- I was stationed in Scotland. I was stationed in Lancashire. I was stationed in the south of England. We moved around quite a lot, up till the time of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] D-Day, I was stationed in Suffolk, England, where we sailed from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they continue any of your education at all in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. Yes. And training. Incidentally, I did forget to mention. But before I-- we went to Europe, I got transferred to the Royal Engineers. They needed, what they call, tractor drivers. But what I found out, tractors were, in Army terminology, were bulldozers. But anyway-- so I became a bulldozer and a mechanical engineer-- mechanical equipment operator.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your function?

speaker: interviewee
text: Function-- to operate bulldozers for building roads, building air fields. So we used mechanical equipment. It was either bulldozers, or scrapers, or face shovels, all that kind of mechanical equipment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to your basic training. You enlisted in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you do your basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: I done my basic training in Mill Hill barracks, Bittacy Hill, which was Hendon, London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that far from where your wife was living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I joined the Army, I wasn't with my wife. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, you got married. And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I think-- well, obviously, we were moved around a lot, you know? We had occasional weekend leaves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did your wife stay with her family? Or did she move into a flat with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Unfortunately, my wife was an orphan. She lost her two parents well before I met her. And she came actually, originally, from Manchester with two sisters. Well, I met her then in 1938, I think. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other members-- other Jewish soldiers in your unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some boy named Ehrlich, who also lived in Hackney with me. Joined the Army same time as me. Was another Jewish fellow called, [? Botchin ?], who joined the same regiment as me. And there was another one, I think, called, David Goldstein. Far as I remember, there was just quite a number of Jewish boys at that time, mainly from the Young Communist League, who joined the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewer
text: Being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As a matter of fact, I found very, very, very little anti-Semitism in the British Army. Very, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any episodes of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just after the war broke out, because I was a regular soldier, like many others, we were transferred to certain sections of the Territorial Army. In 1940, I was transferred to a regiment called, the Princess Louise's Kensington Regiment, which was a company called PLKR. And [? well ?] I found out after-- many of my friend found out-- that-- that basically-- not the best part, but quite a big part of this regiment was made up of Ex-Mosleyites, especially the officers are men, which we-- I wasn't aware of. And I think that was the only time I felt a bit of anti-Semitism. But throughout my whole Army career, there was very, very little anti-Semitism in the British Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Mosleyites do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Mosleyites do to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just spurious remarks. They never done anything physical. I mean, we were always-- I was always having arguments with them about their attitude and their way of thinking. No, it never came to any fistfights or anything like that. They weren't-- you wouldn't-- they wouldn't allow you to in the Army. But I found that a little discouraging. And then I got transferred again. From there, I got transferred into the Royal Engineers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make good friends in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I had quite a few buddies, nice buddies. Yeah. They-- most of them were English boys, Christian boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't treat you differently because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never felt that you were being treated differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. As a matter of fact, I think some of the best years of my life were in the Army, probably enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what did you know about what was happening to Jews in Europe? Did you know anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We read about it in the newspapers. I think, towards the latter end of '43, towards the latter end of, I think news was coming out about the death camps in Germany and what the Jews were going through in Europe under the Nazis. So obviously, we did hear a few things about that. But I don't think we realized about the horrors that were going on in the concentration camps. I think it's only after we actually landed in Normandy that the full picture of what was happening-- what happened there did we realize it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, did your family still have family in Europe? In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. As far as I know, as far as I could ascertain from the rest of my family and from my sisters, that we had two-- we had two aunts who were living in France. And I think there was some family still left in Poland, maybe one or two. But I wouldn't know them. I've never met them. But one--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no family contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I think two of the aunts who-- I think one of them only came once to England prior to the war. Far as I remember, her name was Auntie [? DeVera. ?] But we afterward learned that both died in construction camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- you told me you'd heard about Kristallnacht, 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, quite obviously it was common news. I mean, [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you respond to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I felt like most Jews. I was-- what was happening was pretty terrible. And what could we do? I don't think England at the time was ready to invade or-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: But this helped you to be motivated to join up? This was enough--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- Kristallnacht was before-- well after I joined up. It was mid-1939? No, it was before I joined. [INAUDIBLE] When did that happen? 1938, was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: November, '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: November, '39. No. Well, I was already in the Army. I think we heard something about it. But how could I say this? I probably felt pretty helpless and felt sorry for what was happening to the Jews. But I don't think we realized the enormity of the persecution that was going on there. I think it was played down a lot, quite honestly, in the press.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish press, like a Jewish news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was a a Jewish-- I believe it was called. It was a Jewish newspaper in London. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that make-- have any editorials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Always. Yeah. But I think half of us didn't really realize or really appreciate what was really going on in Germany at the time. We knew there was anti-Semitism then. We knew there was some persecution. But we didn't realize the extent of-- you know, even the concentration camps, we never knew, really-- there was these dozens and dozens of concentration camps in Germany-- what was happening there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your family try and make contact with any of their remaining families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other families that you know try and make contact with European Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I can't remember about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in England till 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Until 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1944. OK. And you were in the various regiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in your various regiments and troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I was in the field regiment, Royal Engineers. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Building roads.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, that was the main function of the Royal Engineers at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel unsafe? Or did you ever feel that you were going to be shipped out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it was inevitable, obviously. Once the war broke out, we knew that we could be shipped anywhere. As a matter of fact, one time, we were, again, ready to go to Africa, North Africa, you know, with the 8th Army. But it never developed. It was cancelled. I think, they-- because they were building up for D-Day. But of course, we didn't know this. We didn't realize that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you think you were going to be doing for the whole of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, I knew that, yeah, eventually, that we would be shipped out either to Italy or to North Africa, to Greece. We-- you know, in the Army, they don't tell you anything. They just say, pack your bags. We're being shipped off. But they never tell you where.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when were you finally given notice that you were going to be shipped out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. May-- April or May, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were put in sealed camps in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was such a lot going on in England-- I mean, I think you had to be there to really appreciate the enormity of the scheme, the plan. But when you saw thousands and thousands of vehicles, tanks, guns, you know, being lined up all over some of the roads, the main roads, and especially in Southern England, you knew something was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there other troops there as well? Where there American troops there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There was thousands and thousands of American troops. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet American soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We did. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I was saying, there was a great big camp at the time, a really enormous, big camp. I mean, we were put into what we called sealed camps. That was, they put you there and you couldn't get out. That was just prior to D-Day. Or a month or so before D-Day, they were all sealed. And you were amongst American soldiers, part of the American Army. So obviously, we knew what was happening. We knew there was going to be an invasion, but where or when, we didn't know. Like I shipped out from Harich the 3rd of June, which was three days before D-Day. And all I remember is that most of the soldiers with me, we-- we went by LST, which was Land and Ship Tank. And for three days, we really didn't know-- we knew we-- there was an invasion, but I think most of us were seasick at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you seasick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Fortunately, I wasn't, but most of them were. I was probably a good sailor. I don't know. But we had three pretty awful days in the English Channel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did it take three days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because they were getting all the ships together, aligning them, putting them in their correct echelons. And I mean, there was over 3,000 ships, 5,000 ships. I don't know. You know, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else was on your ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, they lined up all the ships and gave you a signal to [INAUDIBLE], literally, in mid-English Channel for a few days, or two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like then?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we landed, or during?

speaker: interviewer
text: During.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had two storms, which were very, very bad. And I thought, one time, they'd have to turn us back. We didn't know where we were going to. We didn't know what part of France we were going to. Could have been Calais. Could have been anywhere. Once we were-- I think around about the 5th of June did our commanding officers tell us where we were landing. Because they gave us booklets to have to speak French and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you did for the three days on board?

speaker: interviewee
text: Twiddling your thumbs. You were under a lot of tension. You knew-- I mean, we knew what was going to happen. We knew there was an invasion going on. We were told that, but they didn't tell exactly where we'd land. I think it was on the 5th of June we were told that we're going to land on what they called Sword Beach, which was Arromanches. But that was the only time we really knew where we were going to land on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, before we get to the landing, how many soldiers were on board your boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this-- it was not a boat, it was a landing ship tank that carried tanks, carried mechanical equipment, carried heavy equipment. So I imagine it could have been, maybe, more than 1,000 soldiers in an LST. I never counted, obviously. And you just slept on the decks-- there was nowhere to sleep-- and in the lower hold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did you take your own food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they gave what they called field rations. And they say we had to live on the field rations till we got to landed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the general feeling amongst you and your buddies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anticipation. Expectation. A feeling of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what is the word I want to use-- exultation, you know excitement, I think. I didn't feel any fear. I don't think none of us ever felt fear. I think that, number one, the weather was so bad, when we actually landed, I think we were relieved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that you were finally going to do some actual fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you hadn't done any at all then, had you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You hadn't done any actual fighting yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. None at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought that this could be the time to actually see action?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know. No, I didn't feel anything, to be quite honest. I just didn't feel anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you armed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with a rifle and then a-- and a stinger, which were, quite obviously, damn useless.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been trained to use them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Of course. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us about the landing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my only recollection of the landing, that I was a co-driver to a bulldozer operator. I sat with him. And of course, we were living targets, really, because we were well above the, you know, ordinary level.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember going-- getting off the LST. And I do remember, which always sticks in my mind, all the soldiers dead in the water. Mainly Canadians, funnily enough. They'd just been washed ashore, you know? Because we were the second wave, as far as I remember. We landed at Arromanches I think about 8:00 AM, the first landings, I think, were about 5:00 AM or 6:00 AM. So all we saw was, at that time, just the remnants, whoever got killed from the first landing. But that, I think, that stuck out in my mind. Were you being fired on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, from a distance. Funny enough, well, I recollect there was a-- as you came off the LST, you could just see this patch of sand, the beach, and what looked like houses. In fact, they were block houses disguised to look like houses. And the Germans had their guns in there, and their sharpshooters or snipers, whatever they were. But they were quickly blasted out of the way, you know, or captured.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it scary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Frightening? Did you have time to be frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to be a hero, but I didn't feel anything. I didn't feel anything. I think most of us were relieved that we landed. Because, as I say, we had a pretty bad two days on the English Channel. And I think most of us felt-- we were glad to get on land again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks, Michael. We'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one with the interview with Michael Sheridan. It's the 15th of April, 1996. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
